questions.question: Daily P&L swings on a desk have standard deviation σ = 8 $k. From a sample of n = 64, the standard error of the sample mean is:

explanations.B: 8 is σ itself — you must still divide by √n = 8.
explanations.C: 0.125 divides by n = 64 instead of √n, shrinking the error far too fast.
explanations.D: 64 multiplies σ by √n, which would grow the error as data accumulate.

questions.question: Holding σ fixed, multiplying the sample size n by 4 multiplies the standard error by:

explanations.A: 1/4 is the factor on the variance σ²/n, not on the standard error.
explanations.B: 2 would grow the error; more data reduces it.
explanations.C: Correct. SE ∝ 1/√n, so SE scales by 1/√4 = 1/2.
explanations.D: 4 is the change in n, but SE depends on √n, not n.

questions.question: Holding σ fixed, multiplying n by 9 multiplies the standard error by:

explanations.A: 1/9 is the factor on the variance, not the standard error.
explanations.B: 3 would increase the error; more data shrinks it.
explanations.C: 9 is the change in n; SE scales with √n.
explanations.D: Correct. SE ∝ 1/√n, so SE scales by 1/√9 = 1/3.

questions.question: Increasing n from 25 to 100 (σ fixed) multiplies the standard error by:

explanations.B: 1/4 is the variance ratio 25/100, not the SE ratio.
explanations.C: 2 would enlarge the error; SE falls as n rises.
explanations.D: 4 is the factor by which n grew, not the SE multiplier.

questions.question: To halve the margin of error (σ fixed), you must multiply n by:

explanations.A: Doubling n only shrinks the margin by 1/√2 ≈ 0.71.
explanations.B: Correct. Margin ∝ 1/√n; halving it needs √n to double, so n → 4n.
explanations.C: Halving n would widen the margin, not narrow it.
explanations.D: 8 overshoots; √n only needs to double, requiring n × 4.

questions.question: To cut the standard error to one third (σ fixed), multiply n by:

explanations.A: Tripling n shrinks SE by 1/√3 ≈ 0.58, not to 1/3.
explanations.B: Reducing n would increase SE.
explanations.C: Correct. SE ∝ 1/√n; to get 1/3 you need √n × 3, so n × 9.
explanations.D: 6 does not make √n triple; you need n × 9.

questions.question: Reducing n from 100 to 25 (σ fixed) multiplies the standard error by:

explanations.A: 1/2 would apply if n increased fourfold, not decreased.
explanations.B: 4 is the variance ratio, not the SE ratio.
explanations.C: 1/4 reverses the direction; smaller n raises SE.
explanations.D: Correct. SE ratio = √(100/25) = √4 = 2.

questions.question: A 95% interval has half-width 4 at n = 100. At n = 400 (σ fixed) its half-width is:

explanations.A: Correct. Margin ∝ 1/√n; n × 4 ⇒ margin × 1/2, so 4 → 2.
explanations.B: 1 would need n × 16, not n × 4.
explanations.C: 8 doubles the margin; more data shrinks it.
explanations.D: 4 ignores that quadrupling n halves the margin.

questions.question: Doubling the sample size n (σ fixed) multiplies the standard error by approximately:

explanations.A: 0.5 requires n × 4, not n × 2.
explanations.B: Correct. SE ∝ 1/√n; for n → 2n, factor = 1/√2 ≈ 0.71.
explanations.C: 1.41 = √2 would grow the error; SE shrinks.
explanations.D: 2 is the change in n; SE depends on √n.

questions.question: The standard error scales with sample size as:

explanations.A: 1/n falls too fast; that is how the variance of the mean scales.
explanations.B: √n grows with n, but SE shrinks as data accumulate.
explanations.C: Correct. SE = σ/√n, so it is inversely proportional to √n.
explanations.D: n is the sample size itself, not the SE scaling.

questions.question: To shrink the confidence-interval width by a factor of 5 (σ fixed), multiply n by:

explanations.A: 5× n shrinks width only by 1/√5 ≈ 0.45.
explanations.B: 10 does not make √n quintuple; n × 25 is required.
explanations.C: Reducing n widens the interval.
explanations.D: Correct. Width ∝ 1/√n; for factor 1/5 you need √n × 5, so n × 25.

questions.question: If the variance of the sample mean is σ²/n, increasing n by 4× changes that variance by:

explanations.A: Correct. Var(x̄) = σ²/n ∝ 1/n, so n × 4 gives variance × 1/4.
explanations.B: 1/2 is the effect on the standard error, not the variance.
explanations.C: Variance falls, not grows, as n increases.
explanations.D: 2 would increase variance; more data lowers it.

questions.question: You want SE ≤ 1 with σ = 10. The smallest n that works is:

explanations.B: Correct. Need σ/√n ≤ 1 ⇒ √n ≥ 10 ⇒ n ≥ 100.
explanations.C: n = 1000 works but is larger than the minimum 100.

questions.question: You need a 95% margin ≤ 0.98 with σ = 5 (z = 1.96). The smallest n is:

explanations.D: n = 10 gives margin ≈ 3.1, far above 0.98.

questions.question: As n → ∞ with σ fixed, the standard error of the sample mean approaches:

explanations.A: σ is the spread of individual values; the mean's error keeps shrinking.
explanations.B: There is no floor at 1; SE → 0.
explanations.C: More data lowers uncertainty; SE does not diverge.
explanations.D: Correct. SE = σ/√n → 0 as n → ∞, so the estimate becomes arbitrarily precise.

questions.question: For the data {2, 4, 6}, the unbiased sample variance s² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) equals:

explanations.B: 2.667 divides Σ(xᵢ−x̄)² by n = 3 (the biased/MLE version), not by n−1.
explanations.C: 2 is the sample standard deviation s = √s², not the variance.
explanations.D: 8 is the raw sum of squared deviations Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²; you must still divide by n−1.

questions.question: For the data {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, the unbiased sample variance s² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) equals:

explanations.A: 2 divides Σ(xᵢ−x̄)² by n = 5 (the biased/MLE version), not by n−1.
explanations.C: 1.581 is the sample standard deviation s = √s², not the variance.
explanations.D: 1.667 divides by n+1, which over-corrects; the unbiased divisor is n−1.

questions.question: For the data {10, 20, 30}, the unbiased sample variance s² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) equals:

explanations.A: 66.667 divides Σ(xᵢ−x̄)² by n = 3 (the biased/MLE version), not by n−1.
explanations.B: 10 is the sample standard deviation s = √s², not the variance.
explanations.D: 50 divides by n+1, which over-corrects; the unbiased divisor is n−1.

questions.question: For the data {3, 5, 7, 9}, the unbiased sample variance s² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) equals:

explanations.A: 5 divides Σ(xᵢ−x̄)² by n = 4 (the biased/MLE version), not by n−1.
explanations.B: 2.582 is the sample standard deviation s = √s², not the variance.
explanations.C: 4 divides by n+1, which over-corrects; the unbiased divisor is n−1.

questions.question: For the data {5, 10, 15, 20, 25}, the unbiased sample variance s² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) equals:

explanations.B: 50 divides Σ(xᵢ−x̄)² by n = 5 (the biased/MLE version), not by n−1.
explanations.C: 7.906 is the sample standard deviation s = √s², not the variance.
explanations.D: 41.667 divides by n+1, which over-corrects; the unbiased divisor is n−1.

questions.question: For the data {1, 3, 5}, the unbiased sample variance s² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) equals:

explanations.A: 2.667 divides Σ(xᵢ−x̄)² by n = 3 (the biased/MLE version), not by n−1.
explanations.C: 2 is the sample standard deviation s = √s², not the variance.
explanations.D: 8 is the raw sum of squared deviations Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²; you must still divide by n−1.

questions.question: For the data {6, 10, 14}, the unbiased sample variance s² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) equals:

explanations.A: 10.667 divides Σ(xᵢ−x̄)² by n = 3 (the biased/MLE version), not by n−1.
explanations.B: 4 is the sample standard deviation s = √s², not the variance.
explanations.D: 8 divides by n+1, which over-corrects; the unbiased divisor is n−1.

questions.question: For the data {7, 7, 10, 10}, the unbiased sample variance s² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) equals:

explanations.A: 2.25 divides Σ(xᵢ−x̄)² by n = 4 (the biased/MLE version), not by n−1.
explanations.B: 1.732 is the sample standard deviation s = √s², not the variance.
explanations.C: 1.8 divides by n+1, which over-corrects; the unbiased divisor is n−1.

questions.question: For the data {2, 6, 10}, the unbiased sample variance s² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) equals:

explanations.B: 10.667 divides Σ(xᵢ−x̄)² by n = 3 (the biased/MLE version), not by n−1.
explanations.C: 4 is the sample standard deviation s = √s², not the variance.
explanations.D: 8 divides by n+1, which over-corrects; the unbiased divisor is n−1.

questions.question: For the data {4, 8, 12, 16}, the unbiased sample variance s² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) equals:

explanations.A: 20 divides Σ(xᵢ−x̄)² by n = 4 (the biased/MLE version), not by n−1.
explanations.C: 5.164 is the sample standard deviation s = √s², not the variance.
explanations.D: 16 divides by n+1, which over-corrects; the unbiased divisor is n−1.

questions.question: Dividing the sum of squared deviations by n instead of n−1 makes the variance estimate:

explanations.A: Dividing by the larger n yields a smaller value, so it underestimates, not overestimates.
explanations.B: The n-divisor version is biased; the n−1 version is the unbiased one.
explanations.C: Correct. Deviations are measured from x̄ rather than μ, so Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/n underestimates σ²; E[that] = σ²·(n−1)/n < σ².
explanations.D: It is positive unless all values are identical.

questions.question: Bessel's correction (using n−1) is needed because:
options: it converts variance to standard deviation
estimating μ with x̄ uses up one degree of freedom

explanations.A: n−1 makes s² larger than the n-divisor version, not smaller.
explanations.B: The correction concerns degrees of freedom, not correlation between observations.
explanations.C: Taking √ does that; n−1 corrects bias, not units.
explanations.D: Correct. Replacing μ by x̄ removes 1 df, so dividing by n−1 makes E[s²] = σ².

questions.question: The z critical value for a 95% confidence interval (known σ) is approximately:

explanations.A: Correct. ±1.96 captures the central 95% of the standard normal: P(−1.96 ≤ Z ≤ 1.96) ≈ 0.95.
explanations.B: 1.645 is the 90% two-sided value, leaving 10% in the tails.
explanations.C: 2.576 is the 99% value, capturing more than 95%.
explanations.D: ±1 captures only ~68%, not 95%.

questions.question: The z critical value for a 90% confidence interval is approximately:

explanations.A: 1.96 is the 95% value, narrower tails than 90%.
explanations.B: Correct. ±1.645 leaves 5% in each tail, capturing the central 90% of the standard normal.
explanations.C: 2.576 is the 99% value.
explanations.D: 1.28 is the 80% two-sided value.

questions.question: The z critical value for a 99% confidence interval is approximately:

explanations.A: 1.96 corresponds to 95%, not 99%.
explanations.B: 1.645 corresponds to 90%.
explanations.C: Correct. ±2.576 leaves 0.5% in each tail, capturing the central 99% of the standard normal.
explanations.D: ±3 captures ~99.7%, more than 99%.

questions.question: Raising the confidence level from 95% to 99% (same data) makes the interval:

explanations.A: Higher confidence demands a larger margin, widening the interval.
explanations.B: The critical value changes with the confidence level, so width changes.
explanations.C: The center stays at x̄; only the width changes.
explanations.D: Correct. The critical value rises from 1.96 to 2.576, so the margin z·σ/√n grows.

questions.question: Lowering the confidence level from 95% to 90% (same data) makes the interval:

explanations.A: Correct. The critical value falls from 1.96 to 1.645, shrinking the margin z·σ/√n.
explanations.B: Lower confidence needs a smaller margin, narrowing the interval.
explanations.C: The critical value depends on the level, so width changes.
explanations.D: Changing the level alters width, not bias of the estimator.

questions.question: For a fixed sample, which confidence level gives the widest interval?

explanations.A: 90% uses z = 1.645, the smallest of these, giving the narrowest interval.
explanations.B: Correct. Width ∝ z; the 99% value 2.576 exceeds 1.96 (95%) and 1.645 (90%).
explanations.C: 95% (z = 1.96) is wider than 90% but narrower than 99%.
explanations.D: Width grows with z, which differs across levels.

questions.question: A two-sided 95% interval splits the leftover 5% probability as:

explanations.A: That describes a one-sided interval, not a symmetric two-sided one.
explanations.B: If no probability were in the tails, coverage would be 100%, not 95%.
explanations.C: Correct. Symmetric two-sided coverage puts α/2 = 0.025 in each tail, matching z = 1.96.
explanations.D: 1% each gives 98% coverage, not 95%.

questions.question: The single best interpretation of a 95% confidence interval for μ is:
options: There is a 95% probability that μ lies in this particular interval
95% of the data lie inside the interval
The sample mean is correct 95% of the time
Over many repeated samples, about 95% of such intervals would contain μ

explanations.A: μ is fixed and this interval is fixed; the 95% is not a probability about one realized interval.
explanations.B: A CI estimates a parameter, not the share of raw data points inside the bounds.
explanations.C: x̄ is a point estimate; the 95% describes interval coverage, not the mean being 'correct'.
explanations.D: Correct. The 95% is a long-run coverage property of the procedure: P(interval covers μ) ≈ 0.95 across resampling.

questions.question: After you compute a specific 95% CI of [48, 52], the statement 'P(μ ∈ [48, 52]) = 0.95' is:
options: a misinterpretation, because μ and the interval are both fixed

explanations.A: Correct. Once realized, μ is either in [48, 52] or not; the 95% refers to the long-run procedure, so P = 0.95 about this fixed interval is wrong.
explanations.B: It treats the realized interval as random, which it is not after the data are observed.
explanations.C: Sample size does not turn a fixed realized interval into a probability statement about μ.
explanations.D: Knowing σ does not make μ random inside one computed interval.

questions.question: A 95% CI does NOT mean:
options: the procedure covers μ ~95% of the time
95% of future data points will fall in the interval
the center is the point estimate

explanations.A: This is the correct frequentist meaning of a CI.
explanations.B: Correct. A CI bounds the parameter μ, not individual observations; that statement confuses a CI with a prediction interval.
explanations.C: This is a valid, true statement about CIs.
explanations.D: This is true for the usual symmetric CI.

questions.question: Two analysts use the same data and method for a 95% CI for μ. Their intervals:
options: are identical, since the data and procedure are the same

explanations.A: μ is a fixed unknown constant, not random across analysts.
explanations.B: Same data and method yield the same interval; there is no 5% offset.
explanations.C: Correct. A CI is a deterministic function of the data and method, so identical inputs give the identical interval.
explanations.D: They are directly comparable and in fact equal here.

questions.question: Across 100 independent 95% CIs (each from a fresh sample), the expected number that contain μ is about:

explanations.A: 100 would mean every interval always covers μ, i.e. 100% coverage, not 95%.
explanations.B: 5 is the expected number that MISS μ, not the number that contain it.
explanations.C: 50 corresponds to 50% coverage, not 95%.
explanations.D: Correct. Each interval covers μ with probability ≈ 0.95, so E[count] = 100 × 0.95 = 95.

questions.question: Widening a confidence interval (same data) by raising the confidence level:
options: increases the chance the procedure captures μ

explanations.A: Correct. Higher confidence ⇒ larger z ⇒ wider interval ⇒ higher long-run coverage.
explanations.B: Wider intervals from higher confidence raise coverage, not lower it.
explanations.C: The center x̄ is unchanged; only the margin grows.
explanations.D: No finite interval removes uncertainty entirely.

questions.question: A 95% CI that is very wide most likely indicates:
options: high variability and/or a small sample

explanations.A: Width reflects variance, not bias; an unbiased estimator can still be imprecise.
explanations.B: Correct. Width = 2·z·σ/√n, so large σ or small n widens the interval.
explanations.C: The magnitude of μ shifts the center, not the width.
explanations.D: A wide interval is a valid signal of imprecision, not necessarily a mistake.

questions.question: If your 95% CI for a mean return excludes 0, you can say:
options: the true mean is definitely positive
0 is not among the plausible values at the 95% level

explanations.A: A CI gives confidence, not certainty; it can still miss μ ~5% of the time.
explanations.B: The CI is about the mean parameter, not the fraction of positive observations.
explanations.C: Correct. Values outside the interval are not supported at that confidence; this mirrors rejecting H₀: μ = 0 at α = 0.05.
explanations.D: Excluding 0 implies p < 0.05, not necessarily equal to 0.05.

questions.question: Confidence level and interval width trade off as follows:

explanations.A: More confidence requires a larger margin, not a smaller one.
explanations.B: Width depends directly on the critical value z set by the confidence level.
explanations.C: Width depends on z, σ, and n — not on the value of μ.
explanations.D: Correct. Width ∝ z, which grows with confidence, so more confidence costs precision.

questions.question: Which change narrows a confidence interval without lowering confidence?

explanations.A: Correct. SE = σ/√n shrinks as n grows, so the margin z·σ/√n falls at fixed confidence.
explanations.B: Smaller n raises SE and widens the interval.
explanations.C: Higher confidence widens the interval.
explanations.D: Bias shifts the center; it does not reliably narrow a valid interval.

questions.question: 'We are 95% confident μ is in [10, 14]' is best read as a statement about:
options: the reliability of the method that produced the interval
the probability the sample is valid

explanations.A: μ is a fixed constant, not random within the interval.
explanations.B: Correct. Confidence attaches to the long-run procedure, which captures μ ~95% of the time.
explanations.C: It is not a claim about how much data falls in the range.
explanations.D: It is about coverage of μ, not sample validity.

questions.question: A narrower 95% CI compared to another (same confidence) means:
options: a more precise estimate (smaller standard error)

explanations.A: Narrower at the same confidence means more precision, not less reliability.
explanations.B: Both are 95%; only the SE differs.
explanations.C: Correct. At fixed z, a narrower width means a smaller SE = σ/√n, i.e. more precision.
explanations.D: A narrower interval suggests smaller σ or larger n, not larger σ.

questions.question: An estimator θ̂ is unbiased for θ when:

explanations.A: Zero variance describes a constant estimator, not unbiasedness.
explanations.B: Exact equality every time would need zero error; unbiasedness is only about the average.
explanations.C: Averaging above θ is positive bias, the opposite of unbiased.
explanations.D: Correct. Unbiasedness means the estimator equals the target on average: E[θ̂] = θ, i.e. bias = E[θ̂] − θ = 0.

questions.question: The bias of an estimator is defined as:

explanations.A: Correct. Bias = E[θ̂] − θ; it is 0 exactly when the estimator is unbiased.
explanations.B: That is the realized error for a single sample, not the (expected) bias.
explanations.C: Variance measures spread, not the systematic offset from θ.
explanations.D: Bias subtracts θ from the expectation; it does not add them.

questions.question: The sample mean x̄ as an estimator of μ is:

explanations.A: E[x̄] = μ exactly, so there is no upward bias.
explanations.B: Correct. E[x̄] = (1/n)Σ E[Xᵢ] = (1/n)(nμ) = μ, so x̄ is unbiased for μ.
explanations.C: E[x̄] = μ exactly, so there is no downward bias.
explanations.D: E[x̄] = μ holds for any distribution with a finite mean, not just normal.

questions.question: Using Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/n (divide by n) to estimate σ² gives an estimator that is:

explanations.A: Dividing by n is biased; the n−1 version is unbiased.
explanations.B: It is smaller than σ² on average, so the bias is downward.
explanations.C: Correct. E[Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/n] = σ²(n−1)/n < σ², so it underestimates σ².
explanations.D: Its expectation is σ²(n−1)/n, not σ².

questions.question: An estimator can be unbiased yet still:

explanations.A: Always equaling θ would require zero variance, not implied by unbiasedness.
explanations.B: Unbiasedness does not force zero variance.
explanations.C: Unbiasedness does not by itself determine consistency either way.
explanations.D: Correct. Unbiasedness fixes E[θ̂] = θ but says nothing about Var(θ̂), which can be large.

questions.question: Between two unbiased estimators, the 'more efficient' one has:

explanations.A: Correct. Among unbiased estimators, efficiency is judged by variance: smaller Var(θ̂) is more efficient.
explanations.B: Larger variance is less efficient, not more.
explanations.C: Both are unbiased (bias 0); efficiency compares variances.
explanations.D: Efficiency is about variance at a given n, not sample size.

questions.question: If E[θ̂] = θ + 2 regardless of n, the estimator is:
options: biased by +2 (and the bias does not vanish)

explanations.A: A nonzero constant offset of +2 means it is biased.
explanations.B: Correct. Bias = E[θ̂] − θ = 2, a constant offset that persists for all n.
explanations.C: A fixed +2 bias means it does not even converge to θ, so not consistent.
explanations.D: The expectation exceeds θ by 2, so the bias is +2, not −2.

questions.question: The sample proportion p̂ = X/n (X ~ Binomial(n, p)) estimates p and is:

explanations.A: There is no pull toward 0.5; E[p̂] = p exactly.
explanations.B: E[p̂] = p, so no systematic downward bias.
explanations.C: Correct. E[p̂] = E[X]/n = np/n = p, so p̂ is unbiased for p.
explanations.D: E[p̂] = p holds for every p, not only 0.5.

questions.question: A small constant bias may be acceptable if the estimator:
options: has much lower variance, lowering overall MSE

explanations.A: Adding bias and variance both would raise MSE, not justify the bias.
explanations.B: Ignoring data does not justify a biased estimator.
explanations.C: If it always equaled θ it would be unbiased, not biased.
explanations.D: Correct. MSE = bias² + variance, so trading a little bias for a large variance drop can reduce MSE.

questions.question: Which is true about the standard deviation estimator s = √s²?
options: It is slightly biased for σ even though s² is unbiased for σ²
s is exactly unbiased for σ
s² is biased but s is unbiased

explanations.A: Correct. By Jensen's inequality E[√s²] ≤ √E[s²] = σ, so s underestimates σ on average despite s² being unbiased.
explanations.B: The square root introduces a small downward bias; s is not exactly unbiased.
explanations.C: Jensen's inequality gives E[s] ≤ σ, a downward not upward bias.
explanations.D: It is the reverse: s² (with n−1) is unbiased, while s is slightly biased.

questions.question: An estimator with bias 1 and variance 4 has mean squared error:

explanations.A: 4 ignores the bias² term; MSE adds bias² to variance.
explanations.B: Correct. MSE = bias² + variance = 1² + 4 = 5.
explanations.C: 9 = (1+2)² incorrectly mixes the terms; MSE = bias² + variance.
explanations.D: 3 = variance − bias²; MSE adds the terms, it does not subtract.

questions.question: An estimator with bias 2 and variance 5 has mean squared error:

explanations.A: 7 = bias + variance; MSE squares the bias first.
explanations.B: 29 = bias² ·? no — MSE = bias² + variance = 9, not 29.
explanations.C: Correct. MSE = bias² + variance = 2² + 5 = 4 + 5 = 9.
explanations.D: 3 = variance − bias²; MSE adds, not subtracts.

questions.question: Maximum likelihood estimation chooses the parameter that:
options: maximizes the likelihood of the observed data

explanations.A: Sample size is fixed by the data; MLE optimizes θ, not n.
explanations.B: MLE is entirely data-driven; ignoring data is the opposite of its logic.
explanations.C: The MLE depends on the data and is generally nonzero.
explanations.D: Correct. The MLE maximizes L(θ) = P(data | θ), the probability of the data you actually saw.

questions.question: For X₁..Xₙ ~ Normal(μ, σ²) with σ² known, the MLE of μ is:

explanations.A: Correct. Maximizing the normal log-likelihood over μ gives μ̂ = x̄.
explanations.B: The median maximizes the Laplace likelihood, not the normal one.
explanations.C: μ̂ = x̄ in general, equal to 0 only if the data average to 0.
explanations.D: The range measures spread, not the location parameter μ.

questions.question: For X₁..Xₙ ~ Normal(μ, σ²), the MLE of σ² divides the squared deviations by:

explanations.A: n−1 gives the unbiased estimator, not the MLE.
explanations.B: Correct. The MLE is σ̂² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/n; it uses n, which makes it slightly biased.
explanations.C: No standard estimator divides by n+1 here.
explanations.D: √n is the SE denominator, not the variance divisor.

questions.question: For X₁..Xₙ ~ Bernoulli(p), the MLE of p is:

explanations.A: p̂ equals the observed success fraction, 0.5 only if exactly half succeed.
explanations.B: Variance is not the MLE of the Bernoulli success probability.
explanations.C: Correct. Maximizing Σ[xᵢ ln p + (1−xᵢ) ln(1−p)] gives p̂ = (Σxᵢ)/n, the sample proportion.
explanations.D: 1 − x̄ estimates the failure probability, not p.

questions.question: For X₁..Xₙ ~ Exponential(rate λ), the MLE of λ is:

explanations.A: x̄ estimates the mean 1/λ, so λ̂ is its reciprocal, not x̄ itself.
explanations.B: The raw sum is not the rate estimate; λ̂ = n/Σxᵢ.
explanations.C: There is no square root in the exponential MLE for λ.
explanations.D: Correct. The exponential log-likelihood is maximized at λ̂ = n/Σxᵢ = 1/x̄.

questions.question: The likelihood function L(θ) treats the data as:
options: fixed, and θ as the variable being optimized

explanations.A: Correct. After observing the data, L(θ) = P(data | θ) is viewed as a function of θ to be maximized.
explanations.B: That describes the sampling distribution before data; the likelihood fixes the data and varies θ.
explanations.C: If both were fixed there would be nothing to maximize over.
explanations.D: The data drive the likelihood; they are central, not irrelevant.

questions.question: We usually maximize the log-likelihood instead of the likelihood because:
options: the log changes where the maximum is
log turns products into sums and shares the same maximizer
it removes the need for data

explanations.A: A strictly increasing transform preserves the location of the maximum.
explanations.B: Correct. log is strictly increasing, so argmax is unchanged, and ln∏ = Σ ln makes the algebra and numerics easier.
explanations.C: The log-likelihood still depends fully on the data.
explanations.D: Taking logs does not change bias properties of the MLE.

questions.question: A key large-sample property of the MLE is that it is:

explanations.A: MLEs are often biased in finite samples (e.g. σ̂² divides by n).
explanations.B: The MLE is a function of the data, not independent of it.
explanations.C: Correct. Under regularity conditions the MLE is consistent and asymptotically normal, converging to θ.
explanations.D: The MLE depends on the data and is generally nonzero.

questions.question: For a Uniform(0, θ) sample, the MLE of θ is:

explanations.A: The mean estimates θ/2, not θ; it is not the MLE here.
explanations.B: 2x̄ is a method-of-moments estimator, not the MLE.
explanations.C: θ must be at least the largest observation, so the minimum cannot be the MLE.
explanations.D: Correct. The likelihood (1/θ)ⁿ for θ ≥ max(xᵢ) is largest at the smallest allowed θ, namely max(xᵢ).

questions.question: When σ is unknown and estimated by s from a small sample, the correct reference distribution for the mean is:

explanations.A: Correct. Replacing σ by s adds extra uncertainty, so (x̄−μ)/(s/√n) follows a t distribution, not exactly normal.
explanations.B: Using s (not σ) in a small sample requires the heavier-tailed t, not z.
explanations.C: Chi-square describes (n−1)s²/σ², not the standardized mean.
explanations.D: The binomial models counts of successes, not a sample mean.

questions.question: A t-based confidence interval for the mean uses degrees of freedom equal to:

explanations.A: One df is used estimating the mean, so it is n − 1, not n.
explanations.B: Correct. Estimating μ with x̄ costs one df, leaving n − 1 for the t distribution.
explanations.C: Degrees of freedom decrease, not increase, when estimating the mean.
explanations.D: √n is the SE denominator, not the degrees of freedom.

questions.question: Compared with the standard normal, the t distribution has:

explanations.A: t has heavier, not lighter, tails than the normal.
explanations.B: t depends on df and only approaches normal as df grows.
explanations.C: Correct. The extra uncertainty from estimating σ gives t fatter tails, so t critical values exceed z.
explanations.D: t has finite variance for df > 2; it is not undefined in general.

questions.question: As the sample size (and df) grows large, the t distribution:

explanations.A: t converges to the normal, not the uniform.
explanations.B: t is symmetric; it does not become skewed as df grows.
explanations.C: t stabilizes to the normal; it does not diverge.
explanations.D: Correct. As df → ∞, the t distribution converges to N(0,1), so t* → z*.

questions.question: For a 95% CI from a small sample with unknown σ, the t critical value t* compared with 1.96 is:

explanations.A: Correct. With finite df the heavier t tails push t* above the normal's 1.96, giving a wider interval.
explanations.B: t* exceeds z* for finite df; it is not smaller.
explanations.C: t* equals 1.96 only in the limit df → ∞, not for small samples.
explanations.D: t* depends on df; it is not a fixed 2.0.

questions.question: Using z = 1.96 instead of the larger t* for a small-sample mean CI tends to make the interval:

explanations.A: Using the smaller z makes the margin smaller, so the interval is too narrow, not too wide.
explanations.B: Correct. Since t* > 1.96, using z understates the margin, so the interval is too narrow and covers μ less than 95% of the time.
explanations.C: Ignoring the extra uncertainty from estimating σ leaves the interval mis-calibrated.
explanations.D: The center x̄ is unaffected; only the width is too small.

questions.question: The t distribution arises specifically because we:
options: estimate σ from the data rather than knowing it

explanations.A: If σ were known, the standardized mean would be exactly normal, not t.
explanations.B: x̄ is unbiased; the t arises from estimating σ, not from a biased mean.
explanations.C: Correct. The randomness in s (the σ estimate) is what turns the standardized mean into a t variable.
explanations.D: t matters most for small samples; large samples make it ≈ normal.

questions.question: A one-sample t statistic for the mean is computed as:

explanations.A: Using known σ gives a z statistic; the t statistic uses the sample s.
explanations.B: This omits the √n; the SE is s/√n, not s.
explanations.C: This neither subtracts μ₀ nor divides by s/√n correctly.
explanations.D: Correct. The t statistic standardizes x̄ using the estimated standard error s/√n with df = n − 1.

questions.question: To estimate a population proportion p, the natural point estimate is:
options: the sample mean of the raw labels times n

explanations.A: Correct. p̂ = (successes)/(trials) = x/n is the unbiased point estimate of p.
explanations.B: Multiplying by n gives the count x, not the proportion p.
explanations.C: N is the population size, not an estimate of the success fraction.
explanations.D: 1 − x/n estimates the failure proportion, not p.

questions.question: A poll has p̂ = 0.5 from n = 100. The standard error of the proportion is:

explanations.A: 0.0025 is the variance p̂(1−p̂)/n; the standard error takes its square root.
explanations.C: 0.0707 uses √(p̂/n), dropping the (1−p̂) factor in the proportion variance.
explanations.D: 0.5 is √(p̂(1−p̂)) without the /n, so it omits the sample-size shrinkage.

questions.question: A poll has p̂ = 0.2 from n = 100. The standard error of the proportion is:

explanations.A: 0.0016 is the variance p̂(1−p̂)/n; the standard error takes its square root.
explanations.B: 0.0447 uses √(p̂/n), dropping the (1−p̂) factor in the proportion variance.
explanations.D: 0.02 treats p̂ like a standard deviation over √n, the wrong SE formula for a proportion.

questions.question: A poll has p̂ = 0.6 from n = 100. The standard error of the proportion is:

explanations.A: 0.0024 is the variance p̂(1−p̂)/n; the standard error takes its square root.
explanations.B: 0.0775 uses √(p̂/n), dropping the (1−p̂) factor in the proportion variance.
explanations.C: 0.06 treats p̂ like a standard deviation over √n, the wrong SE formula for a proportion.

questions.question: A poll has p̂ = 0.5 from n = 400. The standard error of the proportion is:

explanations.B: 0.0006 is the variance p̂(1−p̂)/n; the standard error takes its square root.
explanations.C: 0.0354 uses √(p̂/n), dropping the (1−p̂) factor in the proportion variance.
explanations.D: 0.5 is √(p̂(1−p̂)) without the /n, so it omits the sample-size shrinkage.

questions.question: A poll has p̂ = 0.1 from n = 900. The standard error of the proportion is:

explanations.A: 0.0001 is the variance p̂(1−p̂)/n; the standard error takes its square root.
explanations.C: 0.0105 uses √(p̂/n), dropping the (1−p̂) factor in the proportion variance.
explanations.D: 0.0033 treats p̂ like a standard deviation over √n, the wrong SE formula for a proportion.

questions.question: A poll has p̂ = 0.4 from n = 600. The standard error of the proportion is:

explanations.A: 0.0004 is the variance p̂(1−p̂)/n; the standard error takes its square root.
explanations.B: 0.0258 uses √(p̂/n), dropping the (1−p̂) factor in the proportion variance.
explanations.D: 0.0163 treats p̂ like a standard deviation over √n, the wrong SE formula for a proportion.

questions.question: A poll has p̂ = 0.5 from n = 2500. The standard error of the proportion is:

explanations.A: 0.0001 is the variance p̂(1−p̂)/n; the standard error takes its square root.
explanations.B: 0.0141 uses √(p̂/n), dropping the (1−p̂) factor in the proportion variance.
explanations.C: 0.5 is √(p̂(1−p̂)) without the /n, so it omits the sample-size shrinkage.

questions.question: A poll has p̂ = 0.3 from n = 2100. The standard error of the proportion is:

explanations.B: 0.0001 is the variance p̂(1−p̂)/n; the standard error takes its square root.
explanations.C: 0.012 uses √(p̂/n), dropping the (1−p̂) factor in the proportion variance.
explanations.D: 0.0065 treats p̂ like a standard deviation over √n, the wrong SE formula for a proportion.

questions.question: A 95% CI for a proportion p̂ = 0.5 with n = 100 (z = 1.96, SE = 0.05) is approximately:

explanations.A: [0.45, 0.55] uses ±0.05 (the SE) without the 1.96 multiplier.
explanations.C: [0.30, 0.70] uses ±0.20, far larger than the 0.098 margin.
explanations.D: [0.49, 0.51] uses ±0.01, far too small for SE = 0.05.

questions.question: For a proportion with p̂ near 0.5, the conservative SE √(0.25/n) means halving the margin requires n to grow by:

explanations.A: Doubling n only shrinks the margin by 1/√2 ≈ 0.71.
explanations.B: 8 overshoots; n × 4 suffices to halve the margin.
explanations.C: Correct. Margin ∝ 1/√n, so halving it needs √n to double, i.e. n × 4.
explanations.D: The margin depends on n through √n, so it must change.

questions.question: The proportion variance p̂(1−p̂) is maximized (worst-case SE) at p̂ =

explanations.A: At p = 0, p(1−p) = 0, the minimum variance, not the maximum.
explanations.B: At p = 1, p(1−p) = 0, the minimum variance, not the maximum.
explanations.C: 0.25 is the maximum value of p(1−p), reached at p = 0.5 — not the location p.
explanations.D: Correct. p(1−p) peaks at p = 0.5, giving the largest SE √(0.25/n) for a fixed n.

questions.question: Using the conservative p̂ = 0.5, the n needed for a 95% margin of about 0.05 (z ≈ 2) is roughly:

explanations.B: n = 40 gives a margin near 0.16, far above 0.05.
explanations.C: n = 100 gives a margin near 0.10, still above 0.05.
explanations.D: ≈ 4000 overshoots; about 400 already achieves a 0.05 margin.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: about how many seconds are in one (non-leap) year?

explanations.A: ≈ 3 × 10⁵ is roughly the seconds in ~3.5 days, off by ~100×.
explanations.C: ≈ 3 × 10⁹ overshoots by ~100×; that is closer to a century in seconds.
explanations.D: ≈ 5 × 10⁶ is about two months of seconds, too small by ~6×.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: roughly how many heartbeats occur in an 80-year human life?

explanations.A: ≈ 3 × 10⁶ is only about a month of heartbeats, off by ~1000×.
explanations.B: ≈ 3 × 10¹² overshoots by ~1000×; that is far more than a lifetime of beats.
explanations.D: ≈ 3 × 10⁷ is roughly one year of heartbeats, off by ~80×.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: about how many breaths does a person take per day (~15/min)?

explanations.A: ≈ 2 × 10² is only a few hours' worth at most, off by ~100×.
explanations.B: ≈ 2 × 10⁶ overshoots by ~100×; that is closer to breaths in a few months.
explanations.C: ≈ 1 × 10³ is roughly an hour of breaths, far too few for a day.
explanations.D: Correct. 15 breaths/min × 60 × 24 = 21,600 ≈ 2 × 10⁴ breaths.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: how many words are in a typical 300-page novel (~300 words/page)?

explanations.A: Correct. 300 pages × ~300 words ≈ 90,000 ≈ 10⁵ words.
explanations.B: ≈ 10³ is about three pages, off by ~100×.
explanations.C: ≈ 10⁷ overshoots by ~100×; that is dozens of novels.
explanations.D: ≈ 10⁴ (10,000) is about a long short-story, ~10× too few.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: how many liters of water fill an Olympic pool (50 × 25 × 2 m)?

explanations.A: ≈ 2.5 × 10³ L equals the volume in m³, forgetting the ×1000 L/m³ conversion.
explanations.B: Correct. Volume = 50 × 25 × 2 = 2500 m³, and 1 m³ = 1000 L, so ≈ 2.5 × 10⁶ L.
explanations.C: ≈ 2.5 × 10⁹ L overshoots by ~1000×; that is a small lake.
explanations.D: ≈ 2.5 × 10⁴ L is about a backyard pool, ~100× too small.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: how many $1 bills (≈ 0.11 mm thick) stacked reach 1 km tall?

explanations.A: ≈ 10⁴ bills stack only ~1 m, off by ~1000×.
explanations.B: ≈ 10¹⁰ overshoots by ~1000×; that stack would reach ~1000 km.
explanations.C: Correct. 1 km = 10⁶ mm; 10⁶ / 0.11 ≈ 9 × 10⁶ ≈ 10⁷ bills.
explanations.D: ≈ 10⁵ bills reach only ~11 m, far short of 1 km.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: how many times does a car tire (~2 m circumference) rotate over 200,000 km of driving?

explanations.A: ≈ 10⁵ rotations cover only ~200 km, off by ~1000×.
explanations.B: ≈ 10¹¹ overshoots by ~1000×; that is far beyond a car's lifetime.
explanations.C: ≈ 10⁶ rotations cover ~2000 km, ~100× too few.
explanations.D: Correct. 200,000 km = 2 × 10⁸ m; ÷ 2 m per rotation ≈ 10⁸ rotations.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: roughly how many hairs are on a typical human head?

explanations.A: Correct. Scalp ≈ 600 cm² at ~150 hairs/cm² ≈ 9 × 10⁴ ≈ 10⁵ hairs.
explanations.B: ≈ 10³ is far too sparse for a full head of hair, off by ~100×.
explanations.C: ≈ 10⁷ overshoots by ~100×; that exceeds plausible follicle density.
explanations.D: ≈ 10⁴ (10,000) is ~10× too few for a typical head.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: how many minutes are in a (non-leap) year?

explanations.A: ≈ 5 × 10³ minutes is only ~3.5 days, off by ~100×.
explanations.C: ≈ 5 × 10⁷ overshoots by ~100×; that is closer to seconds in a year.
explanations.D: ≈ 9 × 10⁴ is roughly the minutes in two months, too few by ~6×.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: how many grains of rice (~0.02 g each) are in a 1 kg bag?

explanations.A: ≈ 5 × 10² grains weigh only ~10 g, off by ~100×.
explanations.B: ≈ 5 × 10⁶ overshoots by ~100×; those grains would weigh ~100 kg.
explanations.D: ≈ 5 × 10³ grains weigh only ~100 g, ~10× too few.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: how many cells are in the human body (order of magnitude)?

explanations.A: ≈ 10⁹ is far too few, off by ~10,000×.
explanations.B: ≈ 10¹⁸ overshoots the accepted figure by ~10⁵×.
explanations.C: ≈ 10⁶ is wildly too small for a whole human body.
explanations.D: Correct. Standard biology estimates put it near 3 × 10¹³ to 4 × 10¹³ cells.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: how many piano tuners are likely working in a city of ~3 million (classic Fermi problem)?

explanations.A: Correct. ~1M pianos? no: ~3M people / ~3 per household ≈ 10⁶ households; ~1 in 20 owns a piano ⇒ ~5×10⁴ pianos; tuned yearly, ~50/week per tuner ⇒ ~2500/yr ⇒ ≈ 20–200 tuners, order 10².
explanations.B: ≈ a handful is far too few to service tens of thousands of pianos, off by ~100×.
explanations.C: ≈ 10⁴ tuners overshoots; there are not enough pianos to employ that many, off by ~100×.
explanations.D: ≈ 10⁶ exceeds the city's entire workforce; wildly too large.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: about how many hours are in a (non-leap) year?

explanations.A: ≈ 90 hours is under 4 days, off by ~100×.
explanations.B: Correct. 365 × 24 = 8760 ≈ 9 × 10³ hours.
explanations.C: ≈ 9 × 10⁵ overshoots by ~100×; that is closer to minutes in a year.
explanations.D: ≈ 3000 hours is about four months, too few by ~3×.

questions.question: Fermi estimate: how many pages would a 1-million-word manuscript fill at ~500 words/page?

explanations.A: ≈ 20 pages would hold only ~10⁴ words, off by ~100×.
explanations.B: ≈ 2 × 10⁵ pages overshoots by ~100×; that is ~10⁸ words.
explanations.C: Correct. 10⁶ words / 500 words per page = 2000 ≈ 2 × 10³ pages.
explanations.D: ≈ 200 pages holds ~10⁵ words, ~10× too few.

questions.question: A point estimate is best described as:
options: a single number from the sample used to approximate a population parameter

explanations.A: A range is an interval estimate, not a point estimate.
explanations.B: A point estimate approximates θ; it rarely equals it exactly.
explanations.C: A hypothesis is a claim about θ, not a numeric estimate of it.
explanations.D: Correct. A point estimate (e.g. x̄ for μ) is one statistic that approximates an unknown parameter θ.

questions.question: The sample standard deviation s is a point estimate of:
options: the standard error of the mean

explanations.A: Correct. s = √s² estimates σ, the spread of individual values in the population.
explanations.B: μ is estimated by x̄, not by s.
explanations.C: The SE is s/√n; s alone is not the SE.
explanations.D: n is known exactly and is not what s estimates.

questions.question: x̄ = 52.3 computed from a sample is:

explanations.A: A CI is a range, not the single number 52.3.
explanations.B: Correct. A single value computed from the data to approximate μ is a point estimate.
explanations.C: x̄ equals μ only by coincidence; in general it is an estimate.
explanations.D: A p-value measures evidence against a hypothesis, not a central estimate.

questions.question: The standard error of an estimator measures:
options: the spread of individual data points
how much the estimator varies across repeated samples

explanations.A: That is σ (or s); the SE describes the estimator's variability, not raw data spread.
explanations.B: Bias is a systematic offset; SE measures random variability.
explanations.C: Correct. The SE is the standard deviation of the estimator's sampling distribution, e.g. σ/√n for x̄.
explanations.D: Population size is unrelated to the SE formula σ/√n.

questions.question: The key difference between σ and the standard error σ/√n is that:
options: the SE is always larger than σ
σ depends on n but the SE does not
σ measures spread of individual values; σ/√n measures spread of the sample mean

explanations.A: They differ by a factor of √n; only at n = 1 do they coincide.
explanations.B: Dividing by √n makes the SE smaller than σ for n > 1.
explanations.C: It is the reverse: σ is fixed for the population, while the SE shrinks with n.
explanations.D: Correct. σ describes one observation's variability, while σ/√n is the (smaller) variability of x̄.

questions.question: If you increase precision of an estimate, you have:

explanations.A: Correct. Higher precision means a smaller SE, i.e. less sampling variability around θ.
explanations.B: Precision concerns variability, not the systematic bias term.
explanations.C: A larger SE means less precision, not more.
explanations.D: Population σ is fixed; precision comes from n via σ/√n.

questions.question: An interval estimate differs from a point estimate because it:
options: reports a range of plausible values with a confidence level
is the same as a p-value

explanations.A: Interval vs point is about expressing uncertainty, not about bias.
explanations.B: Correct. An interval estimate (e.g. x̄ ± margin) conveys uncertainty, unlike a single-number point estimate.
explanations.C: Both point and interval estimates are built from the sample data.
explanations.D: A p-value is a hypothesis-test quantity, not an interval estimate.

questions.question: Which pairing of parameter and its usual point estimate is correct?

explanations.A: Variance σ² is estimated by s², not by the sample mean.
explanations.B: A proportion p is estimated by p̂ = x/n, not by the sample standard deviation.
explanations.C: Correct. The sample mean x̄ is the standard unbiased point estimate of the population mean μ.
explanations.D: n is the known sample size, not an estimate of μ.

questions.question: Mean squared error decomposes as:

explanations.A: The bias term is squared, not used linearly.
explanations.B: MSE adds bias² to variance; it does not subtract.
explanations.C: The two terms add, they are not multiplied.
explanations.D: Correct. MSE(θ̂) = E[(θ̂−θ)²] = (E[θ̂]−θ)² + Var(θ̂) = bias² + variance.

questions.question: A consistent estimator is one that:
options: converges to θ as the sample size grows
equals θ for every finite sample

explanations.A: Correct. Consistency means θ̂ → θ (in probability) as n → ∞.
explanations.B: That is far stronger than consistency, which is a large-n limit.
explanations.C: Consistency is about convergence, not zero variance at finite n.
explanations.D: An estimator can be biased yet still consistent if the bias vanishes as n → ∞.

questions.question: An estimator can be biased but still consistent if:
options: its bias shrinks to 0 as n → ∞

explanations.A: A persistent nonzero bias prevents convergence to θ, breaking consistency.
explanations.B: Correct. If bias → 0 and variance → 0, then θ̂ → θ, so it is consistent despite finite-sample bias.
explanations.C: Growing variance prevents convergence, contradicting consistency.
explanations.D: A data-free estimator cannot converge to the unknown θ.

questions.question: Between an unbiased estimator with variance 10 and a biased one (bias 1, variance 4), the lower-MSE choice is:

explanations.A: Its MSE 10 exceeds the biased estimator's MSE 5.
explanations.B: 5 ≠ 10, so they do not tie on MSE.
explanations.C: Correct. Biased MSE = 1² + 4 = 5; unbiased MSE = 0 + 10 = 10, so the biased estimator wins on MSE.
explanations.D: Both MSEs are computable: 5 vs 10, so the comparison is clear.

questions.question: The 'efficiency' of an unbiased estimator improves when its:

explanations.A: Efficiency compares unbiased estimators; bias is 0 for all of them.
explanations.B: Efficiency is about variance, not about hiding n.
explanations.C: The magnitude of the estimate does not define efficiency.
explanations.D: Correct. Among unbiased estimators, smaller variance means higher efficiency (e.g. approaching the Cramér–Rao bound).

questions.question: As n → ∞, the sample mean x̄ for a finite-variance population is:

explanations.A: Correct. By the law of large numbers, x̄ converges to μ, and Var(x̄) = σ²/n → 0.
explanations.B: x̄ is unbiased at every n; it does not gain bias.
explanations.C: x̄ converges to μ; it does not diverge.
explanations.D: x̄ estimates μ, not σ.

questions.question: The variance of the sample mean for an iid sample is:

explanations.A: σ² is the variance of one observation, not of the mean.
explanations.B: Correct. Var(x̄) = Var((1/n)ΣXᵢ) = (1/n²)·nσ² = σ²/n.
explanations.C: Var(x̄) shrinks with n; it is σ²/n, not σ²·n.
explanations.D: σ/√n is the standard error (a standard deviation), not the variance.

questions.question: Trading a little bias for lower variance is worthwhile when it:

explanations.A: Unbounded bias would inflate bias², raising MSE.
explanations.B: Raising both can only increase MSE.
explanations.C: Correct. If the variance drop outweighs the added bias², overall MSE falls (the bias–variance tradeoff).
explanations.D: Estimation still requires the data regardless of the tradeoff.

questions.question: The full width of a symmetric confidence interval for the mean equals:

explanations.A: z·σ/√n is the half-width (margin); the full width is twice that.
explanations.B: σ/√n is just the standard error, without the z multiplier or the factor of 2.
explanations.C: 2·σ ignores both √n and the critical value z.
explanations.D: Correct. The interval is x̄ ± z·σ/√n, so its total width is twice the margin: 2·z·σ/√n.

questions.question: A 95% CI for μ is reported as [18, 22]. The point estimate x̄ and margin are:

explanations.A: Correct. The center is (18+22)/2 = 20 and the margin is half the width = (22−18)/2 = 2.
explanations.B: 4 is the full width; the margin is half the width, namely 2.
explanations.C: 18 and 22 are the endpoints, not the center and margin.
explanations.D: These swap and misread the values; the center is 20 and the margin is 2.

questions.question: Method of moments estimates parameters by:
options: matching sample moments to population moments

explanations.A: That describes maximum likelihood estimation, not method of moments.
explanations.B: Correct. You set sample moments (e.g. x̄, sample variance) equal to their theoretical expressions and solve for the parameters.
explanations.C: Sample size is fixed; method of moments solves moment equations.
explanations.D: The first sample moment (the mean) is central to the method.

questions.question: For Poisson(λ) data, the MLE of λ is:

explanations.A: Although Var = λ for Poisson, the MLE solves to x̄, not s².
explanations.B: There is no square root in the Poisson MLE for λ.
explanations.C: Correct. Maximizing the Poisson log-likelihood gives λ̂ = (Σxᵢ)/n = x̄.
explanations.D: 1/x̄ is the exponential rate MLE, not the Poisson mean.

questions.question: A point estimate reported without any standard error or interval is incomplete because it:

explanations.A: A point estimate can be unbiased; the issue is missing uncertainty, not bias.
explanations.B: It is computable; it just lacks a precision statement.
explanations.C: It generally does not equal θ, which is exactly why uncertainty matters.
explanations.D: Correct. Without an SE or CI, you cannot tell how precise x̄ is; uncertainty scales like σ/√n.

questions.question: A 99% CI compared with a 95% CI from the same data is wider because it:
options: must capture μ more often, requiring a larger margin

explanations.A: Correct. Higher confidence uses a larger z (2.576 vs 1.96), so the margin z·σ/√n grows.
explanations.B: It uses a larger critical value, which is why it is wider.
explanations.C: Same data, same n; only the confidence level differs.
explanations.D: Both center at x̄; only the width differs.

questions.question: With a very large sample and unknown σ, using z instead of t is acceptable because:
options: t ≈ z when degrees of freedom are large

explanations.A: σ remains unknown; it is the large df that makes t ≈ z.
explanations.B: Correct. As df → ∞, t → N(0,1), so t* ≈ 1.96 for 95% and the approximation is fine.
explanations.C: It is the sampling distribution of the mean (CLT) and large df, not the raw data, that justify it.
explanations.D: The justification is about t→z, not about bias.

questions.question: Diminishing returns in precision arise because SE ∝ 1/√n, meaning each additional observation:
options: reduces SE by a constant amount
reduces SE less than the previous one did

explanations.A: The reduction is not constant; it follows the concave 1/√n curve.
explanations.B: More data lowers SE; it never raises it.
explanations.C: Correct. Since SE ∝ 1/√n, the marginal drop shrinks: going 100→101 helps far less than 1→2.
explanations.D: Each observation does lower SE, just by a diminishing amount.
